verdict_reasoning: ATG9A is a multi-pass transmembrane protein (the only integral membrane core autophagy protein) that cycles between the trans-Golgi network, endosomes, and the phagophore assembly site. It is not a canonical plasma membrane receptor, but surface proteomics and cell-biology studies have documented that ATG9A transiently visits the plasma membrane as part of its constitutive vesicular trafficking cycle — it is retrieved from the PM via clathrin/AP2-mediated endocytosis. This cycling gives it a documented, if minority, PM pool accessible from the extracellular face. The dominant compartment is intracellular (Golgi/endosome/phagophore), qualifying this as dual_localization rather than a stable surface protein. No clinical cell-surface-directed therapeutic programs are known, and no stress- or death-induced outer-leaflet display beyond basal cycling is established.
predicted_key_uncertainty: The magnitude and stability of the ATG9A PM pool from constitutive cycling — whether wash-resistant enough to count as a genuine binder target — remains uncertain without direct quantitative surface-biotinylation data.
